rationales.achievement: He spent "the rest of the afternoon comparing my draft" to top-journal reviews, showing a strong push to improve performance against high standards.
rationales.security: The choice has clearly destabilized his life: he contrasts the present struggle with "a good apartment, a pension plan" and worries the sacrifices may not pay off.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Ingrid "fine" instead of being honest even though she is "picking up extra client work," which withholds support from a close partner.

rationales.achievement: "Revised twenty-three pages of the literature review" reflects sustained, concrete effort to improve his work and meet demanding standards.
Even after rejection, he frames the situation around "working on this for eighteen months" and trying to "build something," showing continued investment in succeeding.
rationales.security: The entry centers on instability: he has "calculated their monthly burn rate," notices his wife buying cheaper food, and longs for "predictable paychecks."

rationales.benevolence: He stayed after class to walk Willem through the model "step by step," and the moment mattered because it was about "someone else learning."

rationales.security: He "ran the numbers" on savings and has started browsing job postings "just in case," showing active contingency planning to restore stability.

rationales.achievement: He focuses on rigorous standards, noting the work was "sloppy" and recalling that he spent "three weeks revising" after methodological criticism.

rationales.benevolence: He finally "told her everything," and the scene centers on mutual care as they acknowledge the worst case and remain "still together, still figuring it out."

rationales.self_direction: She says the version of herself who doesn't help "feels like a stranger" and wonders if she gives because she is "scared of who I'd be if I stopped," suggesting compulsion rather than freely chosen action.
rationales.security: She would "rather be broke" than keep money for herself and keeps lending despite never being repaid, which actively undermines her own stability.
rationales.benevolence: She repeatedly prioritizes close family needs, asking how she could "just say no" when Diego's girls needed shoes and describing constant efforts to show up for relatives.
Even during "twelve hours of driving," she used her break to pick up tia Elena's medication, directly helping a close family member despite exhaustion.
When the girls offered their piggy bank money, she "told them to keep it," underscoring that her help was for them rather than for repayment.

rationales.tradition: "Made tamales with tia Elena for the holidays" and learned "her mother's recipe," directly continuing a family holiday practice across generations.
rationales.benevolence: She spent "the whole afternoon together" with her aunt in a warm, supportive family interaction.

rationales.security: She plans to "pick up extra shifts" and "skip the new tires for another month," sacrificing her own financial and physical safety to cover her brother's rent.
rationales.benevolence: "He's my little brother; that's just what you do" makes clear that she feels responsible for supporting a close family member even at personal cost.

rationales.security: Her focus is on preventing future harm: "What if next time it's worse?" and she is considering having tia Elena move in so she will be safer.
rationales.benevolence: She feels responsible for her aunt's wellbeing, saying "I should have been there" and seriously considering bringing her into her own home.

rationales.hedonism: She admits taking care of herself feels "selfish" and says she can't remember the last time she did nothing "without feeling guilty," showing active resistance to rest and enjoyment.
rationales.benevolence: The entry centers on caretaking for loved ones: she feels full watching everyone together and says "taking care of other people is easy" because they are "why I do everything."

rationales.self_direction: She says yes to Diego "before I even finished reading" and admits finishing her coffee would feel bad, showing she is not acting from her own chosen priorities.
rationales.benevolence: She immediately agrees to watch the girls after school and frames the change around caring for them rather than protecting her own quiet time.
